name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدَٰفِعُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ خَوَّانٍ كَفُورٍ (٣٨)
```

```english
Allah will indeed defend those who have faith. Indeed Allah does not like any ingrate traitor. (38)
```

```arabic
أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقَـٰتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا۟ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ (٣٩)
```

```english
Those who are fought against are permitted [to fight] because they have been wronged, and Allah is indeed able to help them (39)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِم بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ إِلَّآ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ رَبُّنَا ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلنَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ لَّهُدِّمَتْ صَوَٰمِعُ وَبِيَعٌ وَصَلَوَٰتٌ وَمَسَـٰجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا ٱسْمُ ٱللَّهِ كَثِيرًا ۗ وَلَيَنصُرَنَّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُۥٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَقَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ (٤٠)
```

```english
—those who were expelled from their homes unjustly, only because they said, ‘Allah is our Lord.’ Had not Allah repulsed the people from one another, ruin would have befallen the monasteries, churches, synagogues and mosques in which Allah’s Name is much invoked. Allah will surely help those who help Him. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty. (40)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ إِن مَّكَّنَّـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَوُا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَمَرُوا۟ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَنَهَوْا۟ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْأُمُورِ (٤١)
```

```english
Those who, if We granted them power in the land, will maintain the prayer, give the zakat, bid what is right and forbid what is wrong. And with Allah rests the outcome of all matters. (41)
```

```arabic
وَإِن يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَعَادٌ وَثَمُودُ (٤٢)
```

```english
If they impugn you, the people of Noah had impugned before them and ‘Ad and Thamud, (42)
```

```arabic
وَقَوْمُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَقَوْمُ لُوطٍ (٤٣)
```

```english
[as well as] the people of Abraham and the people of Lot, (43)
```

```arabic
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ مَدْيَنَ ۖ وَكُذِّبَ مُوسَىٰ فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ (٤٤)
```

```english
and the inhabitants of Midian, and Moses was also impugned. But I gave the faithless a respite, then I seized them and how was My rebuttal! (44)
```

```arabic
فَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَا وَهِىَ ظَالِمَةٌ فَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا وَبِئْرٍ مُّعَطَّلَةٍ وَقَصْرٍ مَّشِيدٍ (٤٥)
```

```english
How many towns We have destroyed when they had been wrongdoers! So they lie fallen on their trellises, their wells neglected and their ruined palaces! (45)
```

```arabic
أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَتَكُونَ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ يَعْقِلُونَ بِهَآ أَوْ ءَاذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا ۖ فَإِنَّهَا لَا تَعْمَى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَلَـٰكِن تَعْمَى ٱلْقُلُوبُ ٱلَّتِى فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ (٤٦)
```

```english
Have they not travelled through the land so that they may have hearts by which they may exercise their reason, or ears by which they may hear? Indeed, it is not the eyes that turn blind, but it is the hearts in the breasts that turn blind! (46)
```

```arabic
وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِٱلْعَذَابِ وَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ وَعْدَهُۥ ۚ وَإِنَّ يَوْمًا عِندَ رَبِّكَ كَأَلْفِ سَنَةٍ مِّمَّا تَعُدُّونَ (٤٧)
```

```english
They ask you to hasten the punishment, though Allah will never break His promise. Indeed a day with your Lord is like a thousand years by your reckoning. (47)
```

```arabic
وَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَمْلَيْتُ لَهَا وَهِىَ ظَالِمَةٌ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهَا وَإِلَىَّ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٤٨)
```

```english
To how many a town did I give respite while it was doing wrong! Then I seized it, and toward Me is the destination. (48)
```

```arabic
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٤٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘O mankind! I am only a manifest warner to you!’ (49)
```

```arabic
فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (٥٠)
```

```english
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them will be forgiveness and a noble provision. (50)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ سَعَوْا۟ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مُعَـٰجِزِينَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٥١)
```

```english
But as for those who contend with Our signs, seeking to frustrate [their purpose], they shall be the inmates of hell. (51)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ وَلَا نَبِىٍّ إِلَّآ إِذَا تَمَنَّىٰٓ أَلْقَى ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فِىٓ أُمْنِيَّتِهِۦ فَيَنسَخُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يُلْقِى ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ ثُمَّ يُحْكِمُ ٱللَّهُ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٥٢)
```

```english
We did not send any apostle or prophet before you but that when he recited [the scripture] Satan interjected [something] in his recitation. Thereat Allah nullifies whatever Satan has interjected, and then Allah confirms His signs, and Allah is All-knowing, All-wise. (52)
```

```arabic
لِّيَجْعَلَ مَا يُلْقِى ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ وَٱلْقَاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَفِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٥٣)
```

```english
That He may make what Satan has thrown in a test for those in whose hearts is a sickness and those whose hearts have hardened. Indeed the wrongdoers are steeped in extreme defiance. (53)
```

```arabic
وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَتُخْبِتَ لَهُۥ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهَادِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٥٤)
```

```english
[And also for the reason] that those who have been given knowledge may know that it is the truth from your Lord, and so they may have faith in it, and their hearts may be humbled before Him. Indeed Allah guides those who have faith to a straight path. (54)
```

```arabic
وَلَا يَزَالُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّنْهُ حَتَّىٰ تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ بَغْتَةً أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمٍ عَقِيمٍ (٥٥)
```

```english
Those who are faithless persist in their doubt about it, until either the Hour overtakes them suddenly, or they are overtaken by the punishment of an inauspicious day. (55)
```

```arabic
ٱلْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلَّهِ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٥٦)
```

```english
On that day all sovereignty will belong to Allah: He will judge between them; then those who have faith and do righteous deeds will be in gardens of bliss, (56)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٥٧)
```

```english
and those who are faithless and deny Our signs—for such there will be a humiliating punishment. (57)
```

## (Commentary)

These verses contain the permission granted to the believers to engage in combat. It is said that these were the first verses revealed concerning jihad (الجهاد) — *the struggle in the way of Allah*. For some time, the believers had been asking the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to permit them to fight the polytheists, but he would tell them: "I have not been commanded regarding anything in combat." While in Mecca before the Migration (الهجرة), individual believers would come to him every day—some beaten, some with head wounds, and others tortured through persecution (الفتنة)—complaining to him about what they endured from the tyrants among the Meccan polytheists. He would comfort them and command them to be patient and await relief, until these verses were revealed, including His saying: **أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقاتَلُونَ** — "Permission [to fight] is given to those who are being fought..." (22:39).

As mentioned, these were the first verses revealed regarding jihad. It has also been said that the first to be revealed was His saying, the Exalted: **وَقاتِلُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ الَّذِينَ يُقاتِلُونَكُمْ** — "Fight in the way of Allah those who fight you" (2:190). Others have said it was His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ اشْتَرى مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَأَمْوالَهُمْ** — "Indeed Allah has bought from the believers their souls and their possessions" (9:111).

However, analytical consideration (الاعتبار) requires that the verse from Surah al-Hajj be the one revealed first. This is because it contains an explicit permission (الإذن) and is surrounded by introductory preparation, the stirring of the people, the strengthening of their hearts, and the firming of their footing through the promise of victory—both implicitly and explicitly. It also mentions what Allah did to the unjust towns before them. All of this is characteristic of the legislation of significant rulings when they are explained and conveyed for the first time, especially jihad, which is built upon the foundation of sacrifice and self-offering. It is the most arduous and difficult social ruling in Islam, and the most essential for maintaining the religious society (المجتمع الديني) on its own feet. Conveying such a matter for the first time necessitates an expansion of discourse and the awakening of understandings, as is observed in these verses.

The passage opens by stating that Allah is the Protector (المولى) of the believers who defends them. Then it explicitly states the permission for them to fight, mentioning that they have been wronged. It notes that combat is the means to preserve righteous societies and describes the believers as being fit to establish a religious society that performs righteous deeds (الصالحات). It then mentions what He did to the unjust towns before them and that He will seize them just as He seized those before them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ اللهَ يُدافِعُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ خَوَّانٍ كَفُورٍ** — "Indeed Allah defends those who have faith. Indeed Allah does not love any treacherous ingrate" (22:38). The word "defense" (المدافعة) is an intensive form of "pushing back" (الدفع). "Treacherous" (الخوان) is an intensive paradigm (اسم مبالغة) derived from "treachery" (الخيانة), as is "ingrate" (الكفور) from "ingratitude" (الكفران). What is meant by "those who have faith" are the believers of this nation (الأمة), even if it applied specifically to the believers at that time according to the occasion, because the verses legislate combat and its ruling is not restricted to one group over another; the specific occasion does not restrict the general application.

What is meant by "every treacherous ingrate" are the polytheists. They were excessive in treachery and ingratitude because Allah entrusted them with the trust (الأمانة) of the true religion and placed it as a deposit within their primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — so that by preserving and observing it, they might attain the happiness of both worlds. He made it known to them through the path of messengership (الرسالة), yet they betrayed Him through denial and rejection. He showered them with His apparent and hidden blessings, yet they were ungrateful for them and did not thank Him through servitude (العبودية).

The verse serves as a preparation for the permission to fight in the following verse. It mentions as a preamble that Allah defends those who believe; He repels the polytheists from them because He loves the former and does not love the latter due to their treachery and disbelief. He loves the believers for their trustworthiness and gratitude; thus, He defends His religion which is held by the believers.

He, the Exalted, is their Master (المولى) and Guardian (الولي) who repels His enemies from them, as He said: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَأَنَّ الْكافِرِينَ لا مَوْلى لَهُمْ** — "That is because Allah is the protector of the faithful, and because the faithless have no protector" (47:11).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقاتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا وَإِنَّ اللهَ عَلى نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ** — "Permission [to fight] is given to those who are being fought because they have been wronged, and Allah is indeed able to help them" (22:39). The apparent context indicates that the word "permission is given" (أُذِنَ) is the initiation (إنشاء) of the permission rather than a report of a prior permission. It is specifically a permission to fight, as indicated by His saying: "to those who are being fought" (لِلَّذِينَ يُقاتَلُونَ). For this reason, the phrase "those who have faith" was replaced with "those who are being fought" to indicate the matter for which permission was granted.

The standard recitation is "are being fought" (يُقاتَلُونَ) with a *fathah* on the *ta*, in the passive voice (مبنياً للمفعول); meaning those whom the polytheists fight, because it was the polytheists who desired the fight and initiated it. The preposition *bi* in "because they have been wronged" (بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا) denotes causality (السببية), providing the reason for the permission to fight—that is, permission was granted to them because they were wronged. As for what constitutes this wrongdoing, its explanation is in His saying: **الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ** — "those who were expelled from their homes unjustly..." (22:40).

The omission of the explicit agent (الفاعل) of "permission is given" (أُذِنَ) serves to glorify and magnify the source. Similar to this is His saying: **وَإِنَّ اللهَ عَلى نَصْرِهِمْ لَقَدِيرٌ** — "and Allah is indeed able to help them," where He mentions the "ability" (القدرة) to grant victory rather than its immediate actuality. This contains an allusion that victory is not a matter of concern or difficulty, for it is easy for Him who is powerful over all things.

The meaning is: Permission has been granted—from Allah's side—to those whom the polytheists fight, namely the believers, because they were wronged—by the polytheists—and indeed Allah is able to help them, which is an allusion to the coming victory.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ إِلَّا أَنْ يَقُولُوا رَبُّنَا اللهُ** — "those who were expelled from their homes unjustly, only because they said, ‘Our Lord is Allah’" (22:40). This is an explanation of the aspect of their being wronged: that they were expelled from their homes. The polytheists expelled them from their homes in Mecca without any right that would justify such expulsion.

They did not expel them by physical transport or deportation, but rather they harmed them, went to extremes in their persecution, and intensified the torture and trial (التفتين) until they forced them to migrate from Mecca, to become strangers away from their homeland, and to leave behind their homes and wealth. Some went to Abyssinia and others to Medina after the migration of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Thus, their "expelling" them was their forcing them to leave.

His saying: **إِلَّا أَنْ يَقُولُوا رَبُّنَا اللهُ** — "only because they said, ‘Our Lord is Allah’" is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع); its meaning is: "but they were expelled because they said: Our Lord is Allah." This contains an allusion that the polytheists had deviated in their understanding and apostatized from the truth to the point that they considered a person's saying "Our Lord is Allah"—which is the word of truth—as something that permitted them to expel him from his home.

It has been said that the exception is connected (متصل) and the thing excepted from is "right" (الحق); the meaning would be: "they were expelled without any right except the right which is their saying: Our Lord is Allah." You are well aware that this does not suit the context, for the verse is in the position of explaining that they were expelled from their homes without any right, not that they were only expelled by this right and no other.

Describing the believers with this attribute—being expelled from their homes—which is an attribute of only some of them (the Emigrants), is an instance of describing the whole by the attribute of the part, based on the consideration of their unity and coalition. For the believers are brothers and are a single hand against all others. Describing nations by the attributes of some individuals in the Noble Quran is beyond counting.

His saying: **وَلَوْ لا دَفْعُ اللهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُمْ بِبَعْضٍ لَهُدِّمَتْ صَوامِعُ وَبِيَعٌ وَصَلَواتٌ وَمَساجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا اسْمُ اللهِ كَثِيراً** — "Were it not for Allah’s repelling some people by means of others, monasteries, churches, synagogues and mosques where the name of Allah is much-mentioned would have been pulled down" (22:40). "Monasteries" (الصوامع) is the plural of *sawma'ah*, which is a building with a pointed top usually built in mountains and wilderness, inhabited by ascetics and recluses for worship. "Churches" (البيع) is the plural of *bi'ah*, the place of worship for Jews and Christians. "Synagogues" (الصلوات) is the plural of *salat*, referring to the Jewish place of prayer; it is named after the act performed within it, just as "prayer" (الصلاة) is used to mean the mosque in His saying: **لا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلاةَ وَأَنْتُمْ سُكارى** — "Do not approach prayer when you are intoxicated..." (4:43).

It is also said that it is the Arabized form of *salutha*, which in Hebrew means a place of prayer. "Mosques" (المساجد) is the plural of *masjid*, the place of worship for Muslims.

Although the verse occurs in the position of providing the rationale for the legislation of combat and jihad—the gist of which is that the legislation of combat is only to preserve the religious society from the evil of the enemies of religion who are intent on extinguishing the light of Allah, for were it not for that, religious sanctuaries and divine landmarks would be demolished and acts of worship and rituals would be abolished—

However, the meaning of "Allah’s repelling some people by means of others" is broader than just combat. The repelling of some people by others in defense of the interests of life and the preservation of the uprightness of living is a primordial sunnah (سنة فطرية) — *a natural law* — current among people. All primordial sunnahs trace back to Him, the Exalted. This is evidenced by the fact that the human being, like all other beings, is equipped with tools and powers that facilitate physical force, and then with thought which guides him to adopt means of repulsion and defense of himself or any of his affairs upon which his life and happiness depend.

Repelling through combat is the last resort when other means fail, similar to the proverb "the last remedy is cauterization." It involves accepting the destruction of some for the survival of others and enduring hardship for the sake of ease—a sunnah current in human society, and indeed in all beings that possess some form of selfhood and independence.

Thus, the verse contains an allusion that combat in Islam is a branch of this current primordial sunnah, which is the repelling of some people by others regarding their life affairs. When all of this is attributed to Allah, the Glorified, it is His repelling of some people by others to preserve His religion from being lost.

The demolition of places of worship was specifically mentioned—even though it is known that without this repelling, the foundation of religion itself would not stand and all its traces would be erased—because these places of worship and sanctuaries are the symbols (الشعائر) and banners that point to the religion, remind one of it, and preserve its form in the minds.

His saying: **وَلَيَنْصُرَنَّ اللهُ مَنْ يَنْصُرُهُ إِنَّ اللهَ لَقَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ** — "Allah will surely help those who help Him. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty" (22:40). This is an oath with emphatic stress on His helping those who help Him through combat in defense of the divine religion. Allah has indeed fulfilled His promise; He helped the Muslims in their wars and expeditions, supporting them against His enemies and elevating their mention as long as they helped Him.

The meaning is: I swear that Allah will surely help those who help Him by defending His religion. Indeed, Allah is Strong (قوي), whom no one can weaken and nothing can prevent from what He intends; Mighty (عزيز), invulnerable in His presence, whose realm of glory cannot be transgressed, and nothing equals Him in His authority and kingdom.

It appears from the verse that in previous religious laws (الشرائع السابقة), there was a defensive ruling in general, even if its modality was not explained.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ إِنْ مَكَّنَّاهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتَوُا الزَّكاةَ وَأَمَرُوا بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَنَهَوْا عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ** — "Those who, if We granted them power in the land, would maintain the prayer, give the zakat, bid what is right and forbid what is wrong..." (22:41). This is another description of "those who have faith" mentioned at the beginning of the verses. It is a description of the collective as a collective, without looking at individuals. What is meant by "granting them power in the land" (تمكينهم في الأرض) is enabling them to choose the way of life they desire without any preventer or hinderer.

He, the Exalted, says: It is of their character that if they are empowered in the land and given the freedom to choose the way of life they prefer, they would establish a righteous society in which prayer is maintained, zakat is given, the right is enjoined, and the wrong is forbidden. The specific mention of prayer among the aspects of worship (العبادية) and zakat among the financial aspects (المالية) is because each is the pillar in its respective domain.

Since the description is for "those who have faith" mentioned at the start, and the intent is the establishment of a righteous society, and the ruling of jihad is not restricted to a specific group, then what is meant by them is the generality of the believers of that day, and indeed the generality of Muslims until the Day of Resurrection. This characteristic is theirs by nature; for it is the nature of a Muslim, qua Muslim, to be righteous, even if he is sometimes overcome by external factors.

It does not refer specifically to the Emigrants (المهاجرين) as individuals, whether the verses are Meccan or Medinan—even if the mention of being wronged refers to their expulsion from their homes. This is because such a restriction would contradict the generality of the subject described at the beginning of the verses and the certain generality of the ruling of jihad for them and others.

Furthermore, the righteous society that was first established in Medina and then expanded to include the entire Arabian Peninsula during the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—which is the best society formed in the history of Islam, where prayer was maintained, zakat given, and the right enjoined and wrong forbidden—is certainly included in the verse. The primary cause and the dominant factor in that society were the Helpers (الأنصار), not the Emigrants.

It never occurred in the history of Islam that the Emigrants specifically established a society on their own without the partnership of the Helpers, to establish truth and remove falsehood within it. Unless, of course, it is said that what is meant by them are the persons of the Rightly Guided Caliphs or specifically Ali (عليه السلام), according to the disagreement between the Sunnis and the Shi'a; however, that would corrupt the meaning of all the verses.

Moreover, history records certain actions from the early generation, especially among the Emigrants, that we cannot call the reviving of truth and the slaying of falsehood, whether we consider them to be excused mujtahids (مجتهدين) or not. Thus, the intent is not to describe them as individuals, but the collective as a collective.

His saying: **وَلِلَّهِ عاقِبَةُ الْأُمُورِ** — "and with Allah rests the outcome of all matters" (22:41) is a confirmation of the preceding promise of victory and the triumph of the believers over the enemies of religion who wronged them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنْ يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ** — "If they impugn you, then before them the people of Noah [also] impugned [their apostle]..." (22:42) until His saying: **فَكَيْفَ كانَ نَكِيرِ** — "...so how was My rebuke?" (22:44). This contains a consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that his people's rejection of him is not an unprecedented thing, for nations before them rejected their prophets. It is also a warning and a threat to the rejecters by pointing to the end that befell the nations before them—destruction by a punishment from Allah.

Among those nations mentioned are the people of Noah, 'Ad (the people of Hud), Thamud (the people of Salih), the people of Abraham, the people of Lot, and the inhabitants of Midian (the people of Shu'ayb). He also mentioned the rejection of Moses. It is said that He did not say "the people of Moses" because his people were the Israelites and they had believed in him; rather, it was Pharaoh and his people who rejected him.

His saying: **فأَمْلَيْتُ لِلْكافِرِينَ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ فَكَيْفَ كانَ نَكِيرِ** — "Then I gave respite to the faithless, then I seized them. So how was My rebuke?" (22:44). "Giving respite" (الإملاء) is the granting of time and the delaying of the term. "Rebuke" (النكير) is denial or disapproval (الإنكار). The meaning is: I gave time to the disbelievers—those who rejected their messengers from these nations—then I seized them, which is an allusion to punishment. So how was My disapproval of them for their rejection and disbelief? This is an allusion to the magnitude of the disapproval and the severity of the seizure.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها وَهِيَ ظالِمَةٌ فَهِيَ خاوِيَةٌ عَلى عُرُوشِها وَبِئْرٍ مُعَطَّلَةٍ وَقَصْرٍ مَشِيدٍ** — "How many a town We have destroyed while it was wrongdoing, so it lies fallen on its trellises, and [how many] an abandoned well and [how many] a lofty palace!" (22:45). A town "fallen on its trellises" (خاوِيَةٌ عَلى عُرُوشِها) means its walls have fallen upon its roofs, so it is in ruins. An "abandoned well" (البئر المعطلة) is one empty of those who come to it and draw water. To "plaster" (شاد) a palace means to coat it with gypsum; "plaster" (الشيد) with a *kasrah* means gypsum.

His saying: **فَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها** — "How many a town We have destroyed" is apparently an explanation of His saying in the previous verse: "So how was My rebuke?" His saying: "and [how many] an abandoned well and [how many] a lofty palace" is coordinated (عطف) with "town."

The meaning is: How many a town have We destroyed its people while they were wrongdoers, so it is in ruins with its walls fallen upon its roofs; and how many a well is abandoned because those who stayed by it have perished, so there is no one to come to it or draw from it; and how many a plastered palace whose inhabitants have perished, so their forms are not seen nor their sounds heard. The owners of the wells are the desert-dwellers (أهل البدو) and the owners of the palaces are the city-dwellers (أهل الحضر).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَتَكُونَ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ يَعْقِلُونَ بِها أَوْ آذانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِها** — "Have they not travelled through the land so that they may have hearts by which to exercise their reason, or ears by which to hear?..." (22:46). This is an urging and incitement to take heed from these destroyed towns, abandoned ruins, and lofty palaces left behind by those perished nations, by traveling through the land. Traveling through it may prompt a person to reflect within himself on the cause of their destruction and to recall the arguments regarding that. He would then remember that what befell them only happened because of their polytheism, their turning away from His signs, and their arrogance against the truth by rejecting the messengers. Thus, he would have a heart by which to reason and which would deter him from polytheism and disbelief—this is if he is capable of independent reflection.

If he is not capable of that, taking heed would prompt him to listen to the words of a compassionate advisor who desires nothing but his good, and the exhortation of a preacher who distinguishes for him what benefits him from what harms him. There is no exhortation like the Book of Allah and no advisor like His Messenger. Thus, he would have an ear by which to hear that which guides him to his happiness.

From here, the reason for the alternative between the "heart" and the "ear" in the verse becomes clear, without mentioning "sight." This is because the alternative is actually between independence in reasoning and distinguishing good from evil and the beneficial from the harmful, and between following one who is worthy of being followed. These two are the functions of the heart and the ear.

Then, since both meanings—reasoning and hearing—are in reality functions of the heart, i.e., the perceiving soul (النفس المدركة), for it is that which prompts the human being to follow what he reasons or hears from a compassionate advisor, the heart's perception of that is considered a "seeing" and a "witnessing" by it. For this reason, one who does not reason or hear is considered "blind of heart." This is then emphasized by stating that the true blindness is the blindness of the heart, not the blindness of the eye. For one whose sight is blinded can compensate for some of his lost benefits with a staff he takes or a guide who takes his hand, but the heart has no substitute to find comfort in. This is His saying: **فَإِنَّها لا تَعْمَى الْأَبْصارُ وَلكِنْ تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ** — "Indeed it is not the eyes that turn blind, but it is the hearts in the breasts that turn blind" (22:46).

Making the "breast" (الصدر) the vessel for the "heart" is a metaphorical attribution (مجاز في النسبة). There is another second metaphor of this kind in the speech, which is the attribution of "reason" (العقل) to the "heart," while it belongs to the soul (النفس). This has been pointed out repeatedly.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالْعَذابِ وَلَنْ يُخْلِفَ اللهُ وَعْدَهُ وَإِنَّ يَوْماً عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ كَأَلْفِ سَنَةٍ مِمَّا تَعُدُّونَ** — "They ask you to hasten the punishment, and Allah will never break His promise. Indeed a day with your Lord is like a thousand years of your counting" (22:47). The people used to reject the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) when he informed them that Allah had promised to punish them if they did not believe in him. They would ask him to hasten the punishment out of mockery and to challenge him, saying: "When is this promise?" Allah responded to them by saying: "and Allah will never break His promise." If what is meant by the punishment is the punishment of the Meccan polytheists, then the promised punishment is what they tasted on the day of Badr. If what is meant is that which is decreed between the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his nation by a promised punishment that has not yet descended, then Allah has informed of it in His saying: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ** — "There is an apostle for every nation: when their apostle comes, judgment is made between them..." (10:47).

His saying: **وَإِنَّ يَوْماً عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ كَأَلْفِ سَنَةٍ مِمَّا تَعُدُّونَ** — "Indeed a day with your Lord is like a thousand years of your counting" is a judgment on the equality of a single day and a thousand years before Allah, the Glorified. He does not consider the former short nor the latter long such that He would be affected by the brevity of a day or the length of a thousand years. He does not fear missing anything such that He would hasten the punishment for them; rather, He is Forbearing (حليم) and Patient, giving them respite until they complete the depths of their wretchedness, then He seizes them at the term He has decreed for them. When their term comes, they shall not put it back by a single hour, nor shall they put it forward. Therefore, He followed this by saying in the next verse: **وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَمْلَيْتُ لَها وَهِيَ ظالِمَةٌ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُها وَإِلَيَّ الْمَصِيرُ** — "How many a town I have given respite while it was wrongdoing, then I seized it, and toward Me is the return" (22:48).

His saying: **وَإِنَّ يَوْماً عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ كَأَلْفِ سَنَةٍ** is a rebuttal to their hastening of the punishment, stating that a small amount of time and a large amount are equal to Allah. Similarly, His saying: **وَلَنْ يُخْلِفَ اللهُ وَعْدَهُ** is a consolation and support for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a rebuttal to their rejection of what he informed them of Allah's promise, and their challenging and mocking him.

It has been said that the meaning of "Indeed a day with your Lord" is that one day of the days of the Hereafter in which they will be punished equals a thousand years of the days of this world which they count. It has also been said that it means a day for them while they are being punished with their Lord equals in severity a thousand years of punishment in this world.

Both meanings do not align with the beginning of the verse nor the following verse, as is apparent.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ أَمْلَيْتُ لَها وَهِيَ ظالِمَةٌ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُها وَإِلَيَّ الْمَصِيرُ** — "How many a town I have given respite while it was wrongdoing, then I seized it, and toward Me is the return" (22:48). This verse—as mentioned—completes His saying: "Indeed a day with your Lord is like a thousand years," serving as a witness to the truth of the claim. The meaning is: A small amount of time and a large amount are equal to your Lord, and He has given respite to many unjust towns and delayed them, then seized them after a delay.

His saying: **وَإِلَيَّ الْمَصِيرُ** — "and toward Me is the return" is an explanation of the reason for His not hastening the punishment. Since the return of everything is to Him, He does not fear missing anything such that He would seize the wrongdoers in haste.

It has become clear from what preceded that the verse is not a repetition of His previous saying: "How many a town We have destroyed..." (22:45), for each of the two verses has its own significance.

In this verse, there is a transition (التفات) from the third person to the first person singular, because the discourse here concerns one of His attributes, the Exalted, which is Forbearance (الحلم), and the objective is to show that Allah, the Glorified, is Himself their adversary since they opposed His Prophet.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّما أَنَا لَكُمْ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ** — "Say, ‘O people! I am only a manifest warner to you’" (22:49) until His saying: **أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ** — "...they shall be the inmates of hell" (22:51). This is a command to announce the message through warning (الإنذار) and to explain the beautiful reward for faith and righteous deeds—which is forgiveness (المغفرة) through faith and a noble provision (الرزق الكريم), namely Paradise with its bliss, through righteous deeds—and the evil consequence of disbelief and denial, which is being the inseparable companions of hellfire.

His saying: **سَعَوْا فِي آياتِنا مُعاجِزِينَ** — "strive to invalidate Our signs..." (22:51). "Striving" (السعي) is to walk quickly, which is an allusion to exerting effort in the matter of Allah's signs to nullify them and extinguish their light by "challenging" (معاجزة) Allah. The use of the first person plural is in reality a return to the previous context after the transition in the preceding verse, namely His saying: "I have given respite..." (أَمْلَيْتُ لَها).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ وَلا نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا إِذا تَمَنَّى أَلْقَى الشَّيْطانُ فِي أُمْنِيَّتِهِ** — "We did not send any apostle or prophet before you but that when he had a desire, Satan cast [doubts] into his desire..." (22:52). "Desiring" (التمني) is for a person to mentally project the existence of what he loves, whether it is possible or impossible—like a poor person desiring to be rich, or one without children desiring to have them, or a person desiring to have permanence without end or to have wings to fly. The mental image he takes pleasure in is called a "desire" (أمنية). The root of its meaning is *al-many* (المني), meaning "estimation" or "projection." It is said that it sometimes comes with the meaning of "reading" or "recitation"; it is said: "I desired the book," meaning I read it. "Casting" (الإلقاء) into the desire is to interfere in it with that which removes it from its purity and corrupts its matter.

The meaning of the verse according to the first of the two meanings—that the desire is the desire of the heart—is: We did not send before you any apostle or prophet but that when he desired and projected some of what he wished for—such as the alignment of causes for the advancement of his religion and the people's acceptance of it and their faith in it—Satan cast into his desire and interfered in it by whispering to the people, stirring up the tyrants, and inciting the corrupters. Thus, he corrupted the matter for that apostle or prophet and nullified his effort. Then Allah "abrogates" (ينسخ) and removes what Satan casts, then Allah "confirms" (يحكم) His signs by making the effort of the apostle or prophet successful and manifesting the truth; and Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise.

The meaning according to the second of the two meanings—that the desire means reading and recitation—is: We did not send before you any apostle or prophet but that when he recited and read the signs of Allah, Satan cast misleading doubts upon the people through whispering so that they might argue with him using them and corrupt the faith of the believers. Then Allah nullifies what Satan casts of doubts and removes them by granting the Prophet the success to refute them or by sending down that which refutes them.

The verse clearly indicates the difference between the meaning of "prophethood" (النبوة) and "messengership" (الرسالة), and not in the manner of general and specific absolute (العموم والخصوص مطلقاً) as is famous among them—that the "apostle" (الرسول) is one who is sent and commanded to convey, while the "prophet" (النبي) is one who is sent whether commanded to convey or not. For if that were the case, it would have been necessary for His saying in the verse "nor a prophet" (وَلا نَبِيٍّ) to refer to someone other than the apostle, i.e., one who was not commanded to convey; yet this is contradicted by His saying: "We did not send" (وَما أَرْسَلْنا).

We have already presented in the discussions on prophethood in the second volume of this book that which is indicated by the narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام): that the "apostle" is he upon whom the angel descends with revelation, whom he sees and speaks to, while the "prophet" is he who sees in a dream and is revealed to therein. We derived the content of these narrations from His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ لَوْ كانَ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَلائِكَةٌ يَمْشُونَ مُطْمَئِنِّينَ لَنَزَّلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ مَلَكاً رَسُولاً** — "Say, ‘Had there been angels on the earth, walking about at rest, We would have certainly sent down to them from the heaven an angel as an apostle’" (17:95) in the thirteenth volume of this book.

As for the other things said regarding the difference between messengership and prophethood—such as the saying of those who say that the apostle is one sent with a new law (شرع جديد) while the prophet is more general than him and includes those who come confirming a previous law—this is refuted by the fact that we have established in the discussions on prophethood that the divine laws do not exceed five: the laws of Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), yet the Quran explicitly states the messengership of a large number of others besides these. Furthermore, there is no evidence for this saying.

The same applies to the saying of those who say that the apostle is one who has a book while the prophet is otherwise, and the saying of those who say that the apostle is one who has a book and abrogation in general while the prophet is otherwise. These two are refuted by similar arguments as the first.

In His saying: **فَيَنْسَخُ اللهُ ما يُلْقِي الشَّيْطانُ ثُمَّ يُحْكِمُ اللهُ آياتِهِ** — "then Allah abrogates what Satan casts; then Allah confirms His signs" (22:52), there is a transition from the first person plural to the third person. The reason for this is the concern to mention the Name of Majesty (لفظ الجلالة) and to attribute abrogation and confirmation to Him against whom nothing can stand. For this very reason, He repeated the Name of Majesty a second time, even though it is an instance of placing the explicit noun in the place of the pronoun. Also from this is the repetition of the word "Satan" a second time instead of its pronoun, to point out that the one casting is Satan, who is of no account and whose plot is nothing in His presence. It would have been expected to say: "then He abrogates what he casts, then He confirms His signs."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِيَجْعَلَ ما يُلْقِي الشَّيْطانُ فِتْنَةً لِلَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَالْقاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ** — "that He may make what Satan casts a trial for those in whose hearts is a sickness and those whose hearts are hard..." (22:53). "Sickness of the heart" (مرض القلب) is the lack of uprightness in its reasoning, such that it does not yield to the truth it ought to yield to, which is doubt and skepticism. "Hardness" (قساوة) of the heart is its rigidity and coarseness, derived from a "hard stone" (الحجر القاسي), i.e., solid. Its rigidity is the nullification of its delicate emotions that assist in perceiving true meanings, such as humility, mercy, modesty, and love. Thus, the sick heart is quick to conceive the truth but slow to yield to it, while the hard heart is slow in both. Both are quick to accept satanic whisperings.

The satanic castings that corrupt matters for the truth and its people and nullify the efforts of the apostles and prophets—preventing them from having their effect—even if attributed to Satan himself, they are like all other effects: since they occur within His kingdom, the Exalted, and no effect occurs from an effector nor an act from an agent except by His permission, and nothing occurs by His permission except that it is attributed to Him in some way to the extent of the permission, and nothing is attributed to Him except that which contains good and is not devoid of wisdom and purpose.

Therefore, the Glorified mentioned in this verse that these satanic castings have a wisdom: they are a trial (محنة) by which people in general are tested. Testing is one of the general divine laws (النواميس الإلهية) current in the human world, upon which depends the realization of the happy person's happiness and the wretched person's wretchedness. It is a "trial" (فتنة) — *a test that reveals one's true state* — specifically for those in whose hearts is a sickness and those whose hearts are hard. For the realization of the wretched in the perfection of their wretchedness is part of the divine cultivation intended in the system of creation. He, the Exalted, said: **كُلًّا نُمِدُّ هؤُلاءِ وَهَؤُلاءِ مِنْ عَطاءِ رَبِّكَ وَما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً** — "To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not barred" (17:20).

This is the meaning of His saying: "that He may make what Satan casts a trial for those in whose hearts is a sickness and those whose hearts are hard." The *lam* in "that He may make" (لِيَجْعَلَ) denotes causality, providing the reason for Satan's casting into the desire of the apostle and prophet; i.e., Satan does such-and-forth so that Allah may do such-and-forth. Its meaning is that he is subjugated to Allah, the Glorified, for the purpose of testing the servants and as a trial for the people of doubt and denial and their delusion.

It has become clear that what is meant by "trial" (الفتنة) is the affliction and testing that results in delusion and misguidance; by "those in whose hearts is a sickness" are the people of doubt among the disbelievers; and by "those whose hearts are hard" are the people of denial and stubbornness among them.

His saying: **وَإِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ لَفِي شِقاقٍ بَعِيدٍ** — "Indeed the wrongdoers are in a deep defiance" (22:53). "Defiance" (الشقاق) and "opposition" (المشاقة) mean divergence and disagreement. Describing it as "deep" or "far" (بعيد) is describing it by the state of the one described. The meaning is: Indeed the wrongdoers—who are the people of denial according to what the context provides, or both them and the people of doubt—are in a divergence and disagreement whose possessor is far from the truth and its people.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلِيَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ فَيُؤْمِنُوا بِهِ فَتُخْبِتَ لَهُ قُلُوبُهُمْ** — "and that those who have been given knowledge may know that it is the truth from your Lord, and so believe in it, and their hearts may be humbled before it..." (22:54). What is immediately understood from the context is that it is coordinated with His saying "that He may make" (لِيَجْعَلَ) and is a rationale for His saying: "then Allah abrogates what Satan casts; then Allah confirms His signs." The pronoun in "that it is" (أَنَّهُ) refers to what the apostle and prophet desired, understood from His saying "when he had a desire" (إِذا تَمَنَّى), and there is no evidence for referring it to the Quran.

The meaning is: Allah abrogates what Satan casts and then confirms His signs so that those who have been given knowledge may know, by means of that abrogation and confirmation, that what the apostle or prophet desired is the truth from your Lord—due to the nullification of what Satan cast—and so they believe in it and their hearts "become humble" (تخبت), i.e., soften and become submissive to it.

It is possible that His saying "and that [they] may know" (وَلِيَعْلَمَ) is coordinated with a deleted phrase, and the sum of the coordinated and the thing coordinated with is the rationale for what He explained in the previous verse regarding His making this casting a trial for those in whose hearts is a sickness and those whose hearts are hard.

The meaning would be: We have only explained this reality for such-and-such a purpose and "that those who have been given knowledge may know that it is the truth from your Lord," similar to His saying: **وَتِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُداوِلُها بَيْنَ النَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** — "We make such days rotate among mankind, so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith..." (3:140), which occurs frequently in the Quran.

His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ لَهادِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** — "Indeed Allah guides those who have faith to a straight path" (22:54) is in the position of providing the rationale for the knowledge of those given knowledge being a goal resulting from His act. It signifies that He only did what He did so that they might know that the matter is true, because He is a Guide who intends to guide them, so He guides them through this teaching to a straight path (صراط مستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا يَزالُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْهُ حَتَّى تَأْتِيَهُمُ** — "The faithless will not cease to be in doubt about it until [the Hour] comes upon them..." (22:55). This verse—as you see—informs of the deprivation of these disbelievers from faith throughout their lives. Thus, what is meant by them is not disbelievers in general, since some of them accept faith after disbelief; rather, it refers to a number of the leaders of the Quraysh who were not granted the success of faith as long as they lived, as in His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith" (2:6).

The "barrenness" (عقم) of the day means its being such that it is not followed by another day, which is the day of destruction or the Day of Resurrection. What is meant by it in the verse, according to what the context of the third verse provides, is the Day of Resurrection.

The meaning is: Those who disbelieve will continue in doubt regarding the Quran until the Day of Resurrection comes upon them, or the punishment of the Day of Resurrection comes upon them—which is a day that comes suddenly, giving them no respite to devise any scheme against it, and it is not followed by another day in which what was missed before it could be made up.

He alternated between the Day of Resurrection and its punishment because they will acknowledge the truth upon witnessing either of them, and doubt and skepticism will vanish from them. Allah, the Exalted, said: **قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا مَنْ بَعَثَنا مِنْ مَرْقَدِنا هذا ما وَعَدَ الرَّحْمنُ وَصَدَقَ الْمُرْسَلُونَ** — "They will say, ‘Woe to us! Who raised us from our place of sleep? This is what the All-beneficent promised, and the apostles spoke the truth!’" (36:52). And He said: **وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَلَيْسَ هذا بِالْحَقِّ قالُوا بَلى وَرَبِّنا** — "On the day when the faithless are set before the Fire, [they will be asked], ‘Is this not true?’ They will say, ‘Yes, by our Lord!’" (46:34).

It has become clear from what preceded that qualifying the day once as being "sudden" (بغتة) and once as "barren" (العقم) is to indicate that it is such that no scheme is of use with it and no rectification of what was missed before it can occur after it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ** — "On that day all sovereignty will belong to Allah: He will judge between them" (22:56) until His saying: **عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ** — "...a humiliating punishment" (22:57). It has been mentioned repeatedly that the meaning of sovereignty belonging to Allah on that day is the manifestation of the fact that sovereignty belongs to Him, for sovereignty always belongs to Him. The same applies to similar descriptions of the Day of Resurrection in the Quran, such as the command belonging to Allah on that day, or power belonging to Allah on that day, and so forth.

We do not mean by this that what is meant by "sovereignty," for example, in the verse is the manifestation of sovereignty metaphorically; rather, we mean that sovereignty is of two types: a true, real sovereignty and a metaphorical, formal sovereignty. Things have a metaphorical, formal sovereignty which Allah granted them, yet He, the Exalted, possesses along with that the true sovereignty in the reality of its meaning. When the Day of Resurrection occurs, every formal sovereignty is removed from the thing characterized by it, and nothing remains of sovereignty except its reality, which belongs to Allah alone. Thus, it is a characteristic of the Day of Resurrection that sovereignty on that day belongs to Allah, and so on.

His saying: **يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ** — "He will judge between them" means there is no judge besides Him, because judgment is a branch of sovereignty. Since no one on that day has a share in sovereignty, no one has a share in judgment.

His saying: **فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا** — "Then those who have faith and do righteous deeds will be in gardens of bliss. As for those who are faithless and impugn Our signs..."—and these are the stubborn and arrogant—**فَأُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ** — "...for such there will be a humiliating punishment" (22:56-57). This is the explanation of His judgment, the Exalted.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) that he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was not commanded to fight, nor was he permitted to do so, until Gabriel descended with this verse: **أُذِنَ لِلَّذِينَ يُقاتَلُونَ بِأَنَّهُمْ ظُلِمُوا** (Permission [to fight] is given to those who are being fought because they have been wronged), and he girded him with a sword."

Also therein: "The polytheists used to harm the Muslims. A person with a head wound or a beaten person would constantly come to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and complain of that to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he would say to them: 'Be patient, for I have not been commanded to fight,' until he migrated. Then Allah revealed this verse to him in Medina, and it was the first verse revealed regarding combat."

**I say:** It is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from a large number of compilers of collections, from Ibn Abbas and others, that it was the first verse revealed regarding combat. As for what some of these narrations contain—that it was revealed specifically regarding the Emigrants among the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—if the narration is authentic, it is an interpretation (اجتهاد) by the narrator. This is because, as mentioned, the verse is absolute and it is inconceivable to direct the ruling of combat to specific individuals of the nation by name when it is a general ruling.

A similar argument applies to His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ إِنْ مَكَّنَّاهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ** (Those who, if We granted them power in the land...), and indeed to His saying: **الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ** (those who were expelled from their homes unjustly...), as per what was mentioned in the Commentary.

Also therein: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ** (those who were expelled from their homes), Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "It was revealed regarding the Emigrants and it applies to the Family of Muhammad (آل محمد) who were expelled from their homes and terrorized."

**I say:** Upon this is carried what is in *al-Manaqib* from him (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "It is us; it was revealed regarding us." And in *Rawdat al-Kafi* from him (peace be upon him): "It applies to al-Husayn (peace be upon him)."

Similarly, what is in *al-Majma'* regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَمَرُوا بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَنَهَوْا عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ** (and bid what is right and forbid what is wrong)—from him (peace be upon him): "We are they."

Likewise, what is in *al-Kafi*, *al-Ma'ani*, and *Kamal al-Din* from Imam al-Sadiq and Imam al-Kazim (peace be upon them) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَبِئْرٍ مُعَطَّلَةٍ وَقَصْرٍ مَشِيدٍ** (and [how many] an abandoned well and [how many] a lofty palace!)—they said: "The abandoned well is the silent Imam, and the lofty palace is the speaking Imam."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in *Nawadir al-Usul*, Abu Nasr al-Sijzi in *al-Ibanah*, al-Bayhaqi in *Shu'ab al-Iman*, and al-Daylami in *Musnad al-Firdaws* narrated from Abdullah ibn Jarad that he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The blind person is not the one whose sight is blinded, but the blind person is the one whose insight (بصيرته) is blinded."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): In a hadith: "**النبي الذي يرى في منامه ويسمع الصوت ولا يعاين الملك، والرسول الذي يسمع الصوت ويرى في المنام ويعاين الملك**" — "The prophet is he who sees in his dream and hears the voice but does not behold the angel; and the apostle is he who hears the voice, sees in the dream, and beholds the angel."

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning. What is meant by "beholding the angel," according to other narrations, is the descent of the angel upon him, his appearance to him, and his speaking to him with revelation. Some of these narrations have already been presented in the discussions on prophethood in the second volume of this book.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated with a sound chain from Sa'id ibn Jubayr that he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited *al-Najm* in Mecca. When he reached this place—**أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ اللَّاتَ وَالْعُزَّى وَمَناةَ الثَّالِثَةَ الْأُخْرى** (Have you considered Lat and 'Uzza, and Manat, the third one besides?)—Satan cast upon his tongue: "Those are the high-flying cranes (الغرانيق العلى), and indeed their intercession is hoped for." They said: "He has not mentioned our gods with good before today." So he prostrated and they prostrated.

Then Gabriel came after that and said: "Recite to me what I brought to you." When he reached "Those are the high-flying cranes and indeed their intercession is hoped for," Gabriel said: "I did not bring this to you; this is from Satan." Then Allah revealed: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ وَلا نَبِيٍّ** (We did not send any apostle or prophet before you...).

**I say:** The narration is reported through numerous paths from Ibn Abbas and a group of the Successors (التابعين), and a group of them, including al-Hafiz ibn Hajar, have authenticated it.

However, the definitive proofs for his inerrancy (عصمته) (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) belie its content, even if the authenticity of its chain were assumed. It is mandatory to sanctify his holy presence from such a sin. Furthermore, the narration attributes to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) the most hideous and repulsive ignorance: he recited "Those are the high-flying cranes and indeed their intercession is hoped for" and was ignorant that it was not from the speech of Allah nor brought down by Gabriel; he was ignorant that it was explicit disbelief (كفر صريح) necessitating apostasy (الارتداد), and he continued in his ignorance until he prostrated and they prostrated at the end of the Surah without noticing. Then he continued in his ignorance until Gabriel descended upon him and commanded him to recite the Surah to him, so he recited it to him and repeated the two sentences while persisting in his ignorance until Gabriel denounced it. Then He revealed to him a verse that affirms similar hideous ignorance and scandalous sin for all prophets and messengers, which is His saying: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ وَلا نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا إِذا تَمَنَّى أَلْقَى الشَّيْطانُ فِي أُمْنِيَّتِهِ**.

By this, the falsehood of what is sometimes offered as an excuse in defense of the hadith becomes clear—that it was a slip of the tongue (سبق من لسان) pushed by an interference from Satan due to an oversight (سهو) by him (peace be upon him) and a mistake without noticing. Neither does the content of the hadith, with its detailed account of the event, align with this excuse, nor do the proofs of inerrancy permit such oversight and mistake.

Moreover, if such interference from Satan were possible upon his tongue (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by casting a verse or two into the Noble Quran, security would be removed from the divine speech. It would then be possible that some Quranic verses are from the casting of Satan, and then he casts this very verse—**وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ وَلا نَبِيٍّ**—placing it upon the Prophet's tongue and memory so that he thinks it is from the speech of Allah brought down by Gabriel, just as he thought the "cranes" report was such. He would then use this to reveal some of what he cast—the "cranes" report—as a cover for the rest of what he cast.

Or the "cranes" report could be from the speech of Allah, while the verse "We did not send any apostle or prophet before you..." and everything that contradicts idolatry could be from the speech of Satan, covering what he cast of the verse and nullifying the "cranes" report to hide many of his castings within the Quranic verses. By this, reliance and trust in the Book of Allah would be removed from every aspect, and the messengership and prophetic call would be rendered entirely futile. The presence of the Truth is far exalted above that.
